text:
```json
[
  "Four memory allocation schemes (Paged, Demand Paging, Segmented, and Segmented/Demand Paged) remove the need for contiguous program storage and for entire programs to reside in memory during execution.",
  "Paged memory allocation divides jobs into equal-sized pages, which are loaded non-contiguously into equally sized main memory page frames, with disk sectors also matching this size for efficiency.",
  "The Memory Manager tracks these pages using Job, Page Map, and Memory Map tables, a process that increases OS size and complexity.",
  "Demand paging loads only necessary, equally sized pages from secondary storage into memory as needed, allowing jobs to run with less main memory and requiring a high-speed direct access storage device for efficient page swapping based on defined policies.",
  "Common page replacement policies include First-in First-out (FIFO) and Least-Recently-Used (LRU), with LRU variations like the Clock policy (using a circular queue and reference bits for second chances) and an 8-bit shifting technique that tracks page usage history.",
  "The Memory Manager uses a Page Map Table (PMT) containing status, reference, modified bits, and page frame numbers to manage page swapping and handle page faults, which occur when a requested page is not in memory.",
  "To minimize page faults and speed up processing, the working set (pages directly accessible without a fault) of a job can be loaded into memory, with operating systems tracking and sometimes adjusting these sets, especially in time-sharing environments.",
  "Demand paging efficiently utilizes memory by loading job sections only when needed, which introduced virtual memory, allowing jobs to exceed physical memory size, though it incurs overhead from tables and page interrupts.",
  "Segmented memory allocation divides jobs into variable-sized, logically related segments, which are tracked by the Memory Manager using Job, Segment Map, and Memory Map tables, with main memory allocated dynamically rather than in fixed page frames.",
  "Unlike fixed-size, user-invisible pages, segments are variable-sized logical units visible to the user, and their use can reduce page faults caused by loops spanning multiple pages.",
  "Segmented/demand paged allocation combines the logical advantages of segmentation with the physical benefits of paging by subdividing segments into equal-sized pages, thereby overcoming issues like compaction, external fragmentation, and secondary storage handling.",
  "This combined approach requires four tables (Job, Segment Map, Page Map, and Memory Map tables), leading to increased overhead and time for table references.",
  "To mitigate the time-consuming access of Segment Map and Page Map tables, associative memory (a set of registers in main memory) is used to store information about most-recently-used pages and their main memory addresses.",
  "When a page is requested, searches are initiated simultaneously through SMT/PMT and associative registers; if the associative register search is successful, address translation uses its information, bypassing the slower table lookups.",
  "During execution, a job's SMT is loaded into main memory, its PMT is loaded as needed, and all tables are updated as pages are swapped, with a page interrupt occurring if a requested page is not in memory.",
  "Virtual memory, which involves cooperation between the Memory Manager and processor hardware, enables swapping pages between main and secondary storage, giving users the impression that their entire program is loaded in memory, even if only a portion is.",
  "This system allows jobs to exceed physical memory size, uses memory more efficiently by storing only immediately needed sections, facilitates unlimited multiprogramming, and enables sharing of code and data through dynamic linking of program segments.",
  "Virtual memory with paging allows internal fragmentation but prevents external fragmentation, while segmentation allows external fragmentation but prevents internal fragmentation.",
  "Combining segmentation and paging in virtual memory management eliminates external fragmentation and minimizes internal fragmentation.",
  "Shared segments in virtual memory contain unchangeable (reentrant) code and multiple data areas, one for each user, facilitating code sharing."
]
```
json_data: Four memory allocation schemes (Paged, Demand Paging, Segmented, and Segmented/Demand Paged) remove the need for contiguous program storage and for entire programs to reside in memory during execution.
Paged memory allocation divides jobs into equal-sized pages, which are loaded non-contiguously into equally sized main memory page frames, with disk sectors also matching this size for efficiency.
The Memory Manager tracks these pages using Job, Page Map, and Memory Map tables, a process that increases OS size and complexity.

Demand paging loads only necessary, equally sized pages from secondary storage into memory as needed, allowing jobs to run with less main memory and requiring a high-speed direct access storage device for efficient page swapping based on defined policies.

Common page replacement policies include First-in First-out (FIFO) and Least-Recently-Used (LRU), with LRU variations like the Clock policy (using a circular queue and reference bits for second chances) and an 8-bit shifting technique that tracks page usage history.

The Memory Manager uses a Page Map Table (PMT) containing status, reference, modified bits, and page frame numbers to manage page swapping and handle page faults, which occur when a requested page is not in memory.

To minimize page faults and speed up processing, the working set (pages directly accessible without a fault) of a job can be loaded into memory, with operating systems tracking and sometimes adjusting these sets, especially in time-sharing environments.

Demand paging efficiently utilizes memory by loading job sections only when needed, which introduced virtual memory, allowing jobs to exceed physical memory size, though it incurs overhead from tables and page interrupts.

Segmented memory allocation divides jobs into variable-sized, logically related segments, which are tracked by the Memory Manager using Job, Segment Map, and Memory Map tables, with main memory allocated dynamically rather than in fixed page frames.
Unlike fixed-size, user-invisible pages, segments are variable-sized logical units visible to the user, and their use can reduce page faults caused by loops spanning multiple pages.

Segmented/demand paged allocation combines the logical advantages of segmentation with the physical benefits of paging by subdividing segments into equal-sized pages, thereby overcoming issues like compaction, external fragmentation, and secondary storage handling.
This combined approach requires four tables (Job, Segment Map, Page Map, and Memory Map tables), leading to increased overhead and time for table references.

To mitigate the time-consuming access of Segment Map and Page Map tables, associative memory (a set of registers in main memory) is used to store information about most-recently-used pages and their main memory addresses.

When a page is requested, searches are initiated simultaneously through SMT/PMT and associative registers; if the associative register search is successful, address translation uses its information, bypassing the slower table lookups.

During execution, a job's SMT is loaded into main memory, its PMT is loaded as needed, and all tables are updated as pages are swapped, with a page interrupt occurring if a requested page is not in memory.

Virtual memory, which involves cooperation between the Memory Manager and processor hardware, enables swapping pages between main and secondary storage, giving users the impression that their entire program is loaded in memory, even if only a portion is.

This system allows jobs to exceed physical memory size, uses memory more efficiently by storing only immediately needed sections, facilitates unlimited multiprogramming, and enables sharing of code and data through dynamic linking of program segments.
Virtual memory with paging allows internal fragmentation but prevents external fragmentation, while segmentation allows external fragmentation but prevents internal fragmentation.
Combining segmentation and paging in virtual memory management eliminates external fragmentation and minimizes internal fragmentation.
Shared segments in virtual memory contain unchangeable (reentrant) code and multiple data areas, one for each user, facilitating code sharing.
